narrative_summary:
The MSH2 c.1A>T (p.Met1Leu) variant alters the initiator methionine of the MSH2 protein and has been reported in the context of Lynch syndrome and other mismatch‑repair associated cancers.  It is a start‑codon change that has been catalogued in ClinVar and other databases as a potential disease‑causing allele.
Clinical observations are mixed.  The variant has been identified in a Lynch‑syndrome patient who did not meet Amsterdam II or revised Bethesda criteria, suggesting a possible disease association [PMC10589993].  In two large cohorts of mismatch‑repair gene carriers, the same change was classified as pathogenic and contributed to the reported prevalence of pathogenic MSH2 alterations in colorectal and ovarian cancer cohorts [PMC8864140; PMC3493867].  Conversely, several reports have re‑evaluated the allele and assigned it a variant‑of‑uncertain‑significance status, including a study describing its reclassification from likely pathogenic to VUS in a clinical re‑classification analysis [PMC6355179] and separate investigations of prostate and pediatric myeloid neoplasms that also list it as VUS [PMC10010347; PMC7880998].
Functional testing of the p.Met1Leu substitution in a massively parallel assay of MSH2 missense variants demonstrated a neutral effect on protein function, supporting a benign interpretation for this change [PMC7820803].
In silico predictions from a supplementary analysis of lung‑cancer cell lines classified the variant as likely neutral, consistent with the functional data [PMC9325712].
Taken together, the weight of experimental and computational evidence points toward a lack of functional impact, while clinical reports are discordant, with some studies labeling the allele pathogenic and others assigning it VUS or benign status.  The current balance of evidence therefore favors a benign or likely benign classification, but the presence of conflicting clinical interpretations underscores residual uncertainty and the need for further segregation and functional validation in hereditary cancer contexts.

mentions.quoted_text: The heterozygous germline variant in PMS2 NM_000535.7:c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) (ClinVar accession CVC000142777.15; rs587779333) was reported and classified as pathogenic in ClinVar ( accessed on 20 December 2022). The variant was corroborated by capillary sequencing from PCR amplification of the region of interest using specific primers. The sequence was compared with the PMS2 reference mentioned above (Figure 2a).

Based on the personal and family history of cancer, together with the identification of the pathogenic variant in PMS2, the patient was considered to have LS. The molecular testing set was extended to six family members (five siblings and one son of the proband) to determine if they carried the variant c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) in PMS2. Interestingly, this variant was detected in all six family members examined, among which, a chromatogram of a single family member is exemplified as a representative (Figure 2b). In detail, the molecular testing results of the proband and the family members are shown (Table 1 and Supplementary S2). Because each family member tested harbored the variant in a disease-free status, we examined the presence of the variant in seven healthy volunteers unrelated to the study family; that is, not diagnosed with cancer. The presence of the variant was found in 7/7 of the samples evaluated. A representative chromatogram of an unrelated individual is shown (Figure 2c) and all individuals are detailed (Table 1 and Supplementary S2).

The variant c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) in PMS2 affects the methionine involved in translation initiation of this gene. Functional assays for this variant have not been reported; however, the nucleotide change is predicted to generate a non-functional truncated protein. The immunohistochemistry (IHC) analysis correlates 75% of the loss of PMS2 expression with a pathogenic variant in the same gene. IHC showed the nuclear expression of the PMS2 protein (Supplementary S3). This variant has been informed only in an individual with suspected LS and another individual with a second pathogenic variant of PMS2 and a personal history consistent with MMR deficiency syndrome. The rs587779333 variant has reported the alleles T > A; T > C; and T > G. The reference allele in the Latino population is 100% and 0% for the alternate alleles T and G, respectively, according to the Allele Frequency Aggregator (ALFA) database ( accessed on 20 December 2022). In the gnomAD database, the frequency is the same, but there are no data for the alternative "T" allele. Notably, the Latino population is underrepresented compared with the European, African, and African American populations in both databases ( accessed on 1 August 2022; National Center for Biotechnology Information).

We found a frequency of 100% of the variant in the analyzed individuals, relatives and non-relatives alike, within the study sample. In accordance with these results, we raise the possibility that the c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) variant in PMS2 reported as pathogenic is, in fact, a change of sequence in one of the PMS2 pseudogenes. Multiple sequence alignment analysis (MUSCLE, accessed on 8 July 2022) showed homology to 5 pseudogenes: PMS2P1, P2, P4, P5, and P7 (Table 2, Figure 3a). Subsequently, we determined whether the PCR-amplified region (g. -21 to g. 175, PMS2 NG_008466) was common to these five pseudogenes using the Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (BLAST, accessed on 8 July 2022) alignment algorithm. We found that PMS2P1 is the only pseudogene to share the region of interest with PMS2, with 89% identity. In the region of PMS2 corresponding to the ATG at the start of transcription, PMS2P1 contains the TTG nucleotides (Figure 3b). Finally, we estimated the degree of complementarity of the previously used primers with the sequence of the PMS2P1 pseudogene. Results demonstrated 100% and 80% of complementarity with the forward and reverse primers, respectively, allowing simultaneous amplification of the exon one sequence, which corresponds to the homologous region between PMS2 and PMS2P1 (Figure 3c).

The biological significance of genetic variants and their implications for cancer risk render an essential role in medical diagnosis and treatment. In circumstances where a genetic variant is a matter of controversy or has doubt regarding its clinical consequences, it will be crucial to raise the concern in the appropriate outcome report. In the case we presented, the patient had a history of a partial oophorectomy, so during the initial post-test genetic counseling and in contemplating a molecular result related to LS, the possibility of performing a contralateral-oophorectomy was evaluated according to management guidelines. In addition, the impact influences treatment decisions, with the current evidence of the use of immune checkpoint therapy for tumors associated with LS. For these reasons, we propose further investigating the reported variant c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) in PMS2 and evaluating its reclassification to allow appropriate clinical and surgical management for the patient and family members.

Molecular diagnosis of the variant c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) in PMS2. Up sequence reference NG_008466.1. Sequencing chromatograms for (a) index patient; (b) family extension; and (c) healthy volunteer. Arrow indicates c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) in PMS2; the red rectangle marks the change region c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**).

A schematic diagram of the genomic structure of PMS2 and the pseudogenes. (a) The blue boxes show the 15 exons of the PMS2 gene, whose position is representative. The black boxes show the pseudogenes that match exon 1 and exon 5. (b) Multiple sequence alignment of exon 1; red box denotes c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) in PMS2; asterisk (*) denotes conserved nucleotide in all sequences. (c) Alignment of the PCR amplicon with the reference sequence of the pseudogene PMS2P1. The yellow boxes indicate the complementarity of the forward and reverse primers. The red box indicates the site of change c.1A > T, (p. **Met1Leu**).
Prevalence in family tracts and unrelated healthy individuals of the variant c.1A > T, (**p.Met1Leu**) in PMS2.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article argues that the PMS2 c.1A>T (p.Met1Leu) variant, previously reported as pathogenic, is actually a pseudogene‑derived change found in all tested relatives and healthy controls, and should be reclassified as non‑hereditary/benign.
article_mentions.title: A Pathogenic Variant Reclassified to the Pseudogene PMS2P1 in a Patient with Suspected Hereditary Cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study delineates three distinct mutational subclasses of sebaceous carcinoma—UV‑damage, microsatellite‑instability, and ocular—each associated with specific histopathologic and molecular characteristics.
article_mentions.title: Cell of origin and mutation pattern define three clinically distinct classes of sebaceous carcinoma

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that the MSH2 p.Met1Leu variant was found in a Lynch syndrome patient who did not meet the Amsterdam II or revised Bethesda criteria, illustrating cases missed by clinical screening criteria.
article_mentions.title: Diagnosis of patients with Lynch syndrome lacking the Amsterdam II or Bethesda criteria

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies several gene variants that differ in frequency between complete and non‑complete responders to neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy in locally advanced rectal cancer, suggesting potential genetic biomarkers of treatment resistance.
article_mentions.title: Discriminating Potential Genetic Markers for Complete Response and Non-Complete Response Patients to Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy with Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer

article_mentions.title: The effects of genomic germline variant reclassification on clinical cancer care

article_mentions.title: Impact of Different Selection Approaches for Identifying Lynch Syndrome-Related Colorectal Cancer Patients: Unity Is Strength

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study found that less than 1% of women with ovarian cancer carry germline mutations in mismatch repair genes, including pathogenic MSH2 variants such as p.Met1Leu.
article_mentions.title: Frequency of mutations in mismatch repair genes in a population-based study of women with ovarian cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: Sequential IHC with MLH1 promoter methylation followed by MSI achieved the highest sensitivity (92.3%) and specificity (97.7%) for detecting Lynch syndrome in women with ovarian cancer, outperforming other screening strategies.
article_mentions.title: Performance characteristics of screening strategies to identify Lynch syndrome in women with ovarian cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports DNA repair gene mutations in Nigerian prostate cancer, including the MSH2 p.Met1Leu variant classified as a variant of uncertain significance, underscoring ancestry-linked mutation patterns.
article_mentions.title: Whole-exome Sequencing of Nigerian Prostate Tumors from the Prostate Cancer Transatlantic Consortium (CaPTC) Reveals DNA Repair Genes Associated with African Ancestry

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study presents a massively parallel functional screen of MSH2 missense variants, providing functional evidence that most variants, including p.Met1Leu, are neutral and enabling more accurate classification of VUSs in Lynch syndrome.
article_mentions.title: Massively parallel functional testing of MSH2 missense variants conferring Lynch syndrome risk

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports the MSH2 p.Met1Leu variant in a pediatric therapy‑related myeloid neoplasm case and classifies it as a variant of uncertain significance (VUS) based on ACMG criteria.
article_mentions.title: The acquisition of molecular drivers in pediatric therapy-related myeloid neoplasms

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article primarily investigates KRAS‑mutant lung cancer metabolism and only mentions the MSH2 p.Met1Leu variant in a supplementary table, noting it as a somatic mutation predicted to be neutral, without functional or clinical analysis.
article_mentions.title: Targeting de novo lipogenesis and the Lands cycle induces ferroptosis in KRAS-mutant lung cancer
